name: countess
documentation:
# CountESS 0.0.83

This is CountESS, a modular, Python 3 reimplementation of Enrich2.

## License

BSD 3-clause.  See [LICENSE.txt](LICENSE.txt)

Source code is available at [https://github.com/CountESS-Project/CountESS](https://github.com/CountESS-Project/CountESS) and contributions are welcomed.

## Installing

The latest version of
[CountESS can be installed from pypi](https://pypi.org/project/countess/):
```
pip install CountESS
```

For other options, see the documentation below ...

## Documentation

[CountESS Documentation](https://countess-project.github.io/CountESS/)

## Enquiries

Please contact Alan Rubin `alan@rubin.id.au` for further information or
Nick Moore `nick@zoic.org` for code-related matters.

classes.name: countess.gui.main.RunWindow
documentation:
Opens a separate window to run the pipeline in.  The actual pipeline is then run
in a separate process as well, so that it can be stopped.

classes.name: countess.gui.main._CallbackLoggingHandler
documentation:
This is a tiny stub of a logging.Handler which just lets you
direct LogRecords to a callback instead of a handler, eg:
a method in some other class.

classes.name: countess.gui.main.MainWindow
documentation:
Arrange the parts of the main window, with the FrameWrapper on the top
left and the ConfiguratorWrapper either beside it or under it depending on
the window dimenstions

classes.name: countess.gui.tree.FlippyCanvas
documentation:
A canvas which flips all its children's X and Y
coordinates when it goes from portrait to landscape.
Place children with .place(relx=, rely=) for best results.

methods.name: countess.gui.tree.GraphWrapper.on_delete
documentation:
<Delete> disconnects a node from the graph, connects it parents to its children,
and deletes the node.  <Shift-Delete> removes and deletes the node, but doesn't
reconnect parents and children.  <Ctrl-Delete> disconnects the node but doesn't
delete it.

functions.name: countess.gui.tabular.format_value
documentation:
Format value for display in a table:
\>>> format_value(None, "%s")
'—'
\>>> format_value(107, "%4d")
' 107'
\>>> format_value(1.23, "%15.12f")
' 1.23'
\>>> format_value("foo", "%15.12f")
'foo'

very efficient!  There's got to be a nicer way of
detecting this surely?

A frame for displaying a pandas (or similar) dataframe.
Columns are displayed as individual tk.Text widgets which seems to be relatively efficient
as they only hold the currently displayed rows.  Tested up to a million or so rows.

classes.name: countess.plugins.fastq.LoadFastqPlugin

Load counts from one or more FASTQ files, by first building a dask dataframe of raw sequences
with count=1 and then grouping by sequence and summing counts.  It supports counting
in multiple columns.

classes.name: countess.core.pipeline.SentinelQueue
documentation:
This is an easy and a bit lazy way of making a queue iterable.
The writer is expected to call `queue.finish()` when it is done and
the reader can treat the queue like an iterable.

modules.name: countess.utils.pandas
documentation: pandas utility functions

functions.name: countess.utils.pandas.collect_dataframes
documentation:
Takes an iterable of dataframes, ignores any empty ones and tries to pack smaller ones
together up to `preferred_size`.

functions.name: countess.utils.pandas.get_all_indexes
documentation:
Indexes work a couple of different ways, so this massages them into a consistent
dictionary of name => dtype.  A single RangeIndex or a nameless index isn't counted.

functions.name: countess.utils.pandas.concat_dataframes
documentation: handles the special case where there's an empty list

functions.name: countess.utils.pandas.flatten_columns
documentation:
used to rename columns with tuple names like `('things', 'count')` to easier-to-refer-to-
in-python names like `'things__count'`.

modules.name: countess.utils.variant
documentation: Helper functions which find HGVS variants from sequences

functions.name: countess.utils.variant.translate_aa
documentation:
translate a sequence of single-letter amino acid codes
to a sequence of three-letter amindo acid codes

```pycon
>>> translate_aa("HYPERSENSITIVITIES")
'HisTyrProGluArgSerGluAsnSerIleThrIleValIleThrIleGluSer'
```

```pycon
>>> translate_aa("SYZYGY")
Traceback (most recent call last):
    ...
ValueError: Invalid AA Sequence
```

functions.name: countess.utils.variant.search_for_sequence
documentation:
look for a copy of `var_seq` within `ref_seq` and if found return a reference
in the form "offset1_offset2" otherwise return the `var_seq` itself. Don't bother
searching if the length of var_seq is less than MIN_SEARCH_LENGTH.

```pycon
>>> search_for_sequence("GGGGGGG", "CAT", 1)
'CAT'
```

```pycon
>>> search_for_sequence("GGCATGG", "CAT", 1)
'3_5'
```

```pycon
>>> search_for_sequence("GGATGAA", "CAT", 1)
'3_5inv'
```

```pycon
>>> search_for_sequence("CATCATC", "CAT", 4)
'CAT'
```

functions.name: countess.utils.variant.find_variant_dna
documentation:
finds HGVS variants between DNA sequences in ref_seq and var_seq.
<https://varnomen.hgvs.org/recommendations/DNA/variant/insertion/>
Doesn't look for things like complex insertions (yet)

<https://varnomen.hgvs.org/bg-material/numbering/> is pretty clear
that numbering of nucleotides starts at `1`.

SUBSTITUTION OF SINGLE NUCLEOTIDES (checked against Enrich2)

```pycon
>>> list(find_variant_dna("AGAAGTAGAGG", "TGAAGTAGAGG"))
['1A>T']
>>> list(find_variant_dna("AGAAGTAGAGG", "AGAAGTTGTGG"))
['7A>T', '9A>T']
>>> list(find_variant_dna("AGAAGTAGAGG", "ATAAGAAGAGG"))
['2G>T', '6T>A']
```

DUPLICATION OF NUCLEOTIDES

(examples from <https://varnomen.hgvs.org/recommendations/DNA/variant/duplication/> )

```pycon
>>> list(find_variant_dna(        "ATGCTTTGGTGGGAAGAAGTAGAGGACTGTTATGAAAGAGAAGATGTTCAAAAGAA",         "ATGCTTTGGTGGGAAGAAGTTAGAGGACTGTTATGAAAGAGAAGATGTTCAAAAGAA"))
['20dup']
>>> list(find_variant_dna(        "ATGCTTTGGTGGGAAGAAGTAGAGGACTGTTATGAAAGAGAAGATGTTCAAAAGAA",         "ATGCTTTGGTGGGAAGAAGTAGATAGAGGACTGTTATGAAAGAGAAGATGTTCAAAAGAA"))
['20_23dup']
```

(checked with Enrich2 ... it comes up with `g.6dupT` for the first
and g.12dupA for the second, but that trailing symbol is not how it
is in the HGVS docs. The multi-nucleotide ones it gets very different
answers for)

```pycon
>>> list(find_variant_dna("AGAAGTAGAGG", "AGAAGTTAGAGG"))
['6dup']
>>> list(find_variant_dna("ATTGAAAAAAAATTAG", "ATTGAAAAAAAAATTAG"))
['12dup']
>>> list(find_variant_dna("AGAAGTAGAGG", "AGAAGTAGATAGAGG"))
['6_9dup']
>>> list(find_variant_dna("AAAACTAAAA", "AAAACTCTAAAA"))
['5_6dup']
>>> list(find_variant_dna("AAAACTGAAAA", "AAAACTGCTGAAAA"))
['5_7dup']
>>> list(find_variant_dna("AAAACTGAAAA", "AAAACTGTGAAAA"))
['6_7dup']
```

It should always pick the 3'-most copy to identify as
a duplicate

```pycon
>>> list(find_variant_dna("ATGGCCGCCAGCCAA", "ATGGCCGCCGCCAGCCAA"))
['7_9dup']
```

INSERTION OF NUCLEOTIDES

```pycon
>>> list(find_variant_dna("AGAAGTAGAGG", "AGAAGTCAGAGG"))
['6_7insC']
>>> list(find_variant_dna("AGAAGTAGAGG", "AGAAGTCATAGAGG"))
['6_7insCAT']
```

> 0 1
> 12345678901234
> AGAAGTAGAGG
> AGAAGTGAAAGAGG
>
> > ^^^ ^^^

GAA appears earlier in the sequence, but it's too short to bother
including as a reference.

```pycon
>>> list(find_variant_dna("AGAAGTAGAGG", "AGAAGTGAAAGAGG"))
['6_7insGAA']
```

the sequence CTTTTTTTTT is long enough to use a reference for.

```pycon
>>> list(find_variant_dna("ACTTTTTTTTTAA", "ACTTTTTTTTTACTTTTTTTTTA"))
['12_13ins2_11']
```

the most 3'ward copy of the sequence should be the one which is
referred to.

```pycon
>>> list(find_variant_dna(        "GGCATCATCATCATGGCATCATCATCATGGGG",         "GGCATCATCATCATGGCATCATCATCATGGCATCATCATCATGG"))
['30_31ins17_28']
```

```pycon
>>> list(find_variant_dna(        "GGCATCATCATCATGGCATCATCATCATGGGGCATCATCATCATGG",         "GGCATCATCATCATGGCATCATCATCATGGCATCATCATCATGGCATCATCATCATGG"))
['30_31ins33_44']
```

(example from Q&A on <https://varnomen.hgvs.org/recommendations/DNA/variant/insertion/> )
"How should I describe the change ATCGATCGATCGATCGAGGGTCCC to
ATCGATCGATCGATCGAATCGATCGATCGGGTCCC? The fact that the inserted sequence
(ATCGATCGATCG) is present in the original sequence suggests it derives
from a duplicative event."
"The variant should be described as an insertion; g.17_18ins5_16."

> 0 1 2 3
> 1234567890123456789012345678901234
> ATCGATCGATCGATCGAGGGTCCC
> ATCGATCGATCGATCGAATCGATCGATCGGGTCCC
>
> > ^^^^^^^^^^^ ^^^^^^^^^^^

right so the duplicated part is 5_15 and its inserted between 17 and 18.
I think the example in the Q&A is wrong.

```pycon
>>> list(find_variant_dna(        "ATCGATCGATCGATCGAGGGTCCC",         "ATCGATCGATCGATCGAATCGATCGATCGGGTCCC"))
['17_18ins5_15']
```

```pycon
>>> list(find_variant_dna("AAAAAAAAAACCCGGGGGGGGGGTTT", "AAAAAAAAAACCCAAAAAAAAAATTT"))
['14_23delins1_10']
```

DELETION OF NUCLEOTIDES (checked against Enrich2)
enrich2 has g.5_5del for the first one which isn't correct though

```pycon
>>> list(find_variant_dna("AGAAGTAGAGG", "AGAATAGAGG"))
['5del']
>>> list(find_variant_dna("AGAAGTAGAGG", "AGAAAGAGG"))
['5_6del']
```

INVERSIONS

```pycon
>>> list(find_variant_dna("AAACCCTTT", "AAAGGGTTT"))
['4_6inv']
```

OFFSETS

```pycon
>>> list(find_variant_dna("AGAAGTAGAGG", "ATAAGAAGAGG", 100))
['102G>T', '106T>A']
```

```pycon
>>> list(find_variant_dna("AGAAGTAGAGG", "ATAAGAAGAGG", -200))
['198G>T', '194T>A']
```

functions.name: countess.utils.variant.find_variant_protein
documentation:
Find changes between two DNA sequences, expressed
as amino acid changes per HGVS standard.

identical sequences:

```pycon
>>> list(find_variant_protein("ATGGTTGGTTCA", "ATGGTTGGTTCA"))
[]
```

a single AA substitution:

```pycon
>>> list(find_variant_protein("ATGGTTGGTTCA", "ATGGTTCCATCA"))
['Gly3Pro']
```

a single AA deletion:

```pycon
>>> list(find_variant_protein("ATGGTTGGTTCA", "ATGGGTTCA"))
['Val2del']
```

a double AA deletion:

```pycon
>>> list(find_variant_protein("ATGGTTGGTTCAGGC", "ATGTCAGGC"))
['Val2_Gly3del']
```

a single AA duplication:

```pycon
>>> list(find_variant_protein("ATGGTTGGTTCA", "ATGGTTGGTGGTTCA"))
['Gly3dup']
```

a double AA duplication:

```pycon
>>> list(find_variant_protein("ATGGTTGGTTCA", "ATGGTTGGTGTTGGTTCA"))
['Val2_Gly3dup']
```

a single AA insertion
\>>> list(find_variant_protein("ATGGTTGGTTCA", "ATGGTTGGTAAATCA"))
['Gly3_Ser4insLys']

two substitutions are coded as a delins:

```pycon
>>> list(find_variant_protein("ATGGTTGGTTCA", "ATGGCTGCTTCA"))
['Val2_Gly3delinsAlaAla']
```

TODO: this isn't quite correct according to
<https://hgvs-nomenclature.org/stable/recommendations/protein/extension/>

```pycon
>>> list(find_variant_protein("ATGGTTGGTTCA", "ATGGTTGGTTCAAAACAG"))
['Ser4extLysGln']
```

Protein calling should stop at the first Ter encountered:

```pycon
>>> list(find_variant_protein("ATGGTTGGTTCA", "ATGGTTTAGACA"))
['Gly3Ter']
```

```pycon
>>> list(find_variant_protein("ATGGTTTGGTAG", "ATGGTTTAGTAG"))
['Trp3Ter']
```

Call specific synonyms:

```pycon
>>> list(find_variant_protein("ATGGCCTAA", "ATGGCGTAA"))
['Ala2=']
```

```pycon
>>> list(find_variant_protein("ATGGCCAAACCCTAA", "ATGGCGAAGCCATAA"))
['Ala2_Pro4=']
```

```pycon
>>> list(find_variant_protein("ATGGCCAAACCCTAA", "ATGGCGAATCCATAA"))
['Ala2=', 'Lys3Asn', 'Pro4=']
```

```pycon
>>> list(find_variant_protein("ATGGCCCCCAAATAA", "ATGGCGCCAAATTAA"))
['Ala2_Pro3=', 'Lys4Asn']
```

functions.name: countess.utils.variant.find_variant_string
documentation:
As above, but returns a single string instead of a generator

MULTIPLE VARIATIONS

```pycon
>>> find_variant_string("g.", "GATTACA", "GATTACA")
'g.='
>>> find_variant_string("g.", "GATTACA", "GTTTACA")
'g.2A>T'
>>> find_variant_string("g.", "GATTACA", "GTTTAGA")
'g.[2A>T;6C>G]'
>>> find_variant_string("g.", "GATTACA", "GTTCAGA")
'g.[2A>T;4T>C;6C>G]'
```

```pycon
>>> find_variant_string("g.", "ATGGTTGGTTC", "ATGGTTGGTGGTTC")
'g.7_9dup'
>>> find_variant_string("g.", "ATGGTTGGTTCG", "ATGGTTGGTGGTTC")
'g.[7_9dup;12del]'
>>> find_variant_string("g.", "ATGGTTGGTTC", "ATGGTTGGTGGTTCG")
'g.[7_9dup;11_12insG]'
```

PROTEINS

```pycon
>>> find_variant_string("p.", "ATGGTTGGTTCA", "ATGGTTGGTTCA")
'p.='
```

```pycon
>>> find_variant_string("p.", "ATGGTTGGTTCA", "ATGGTTCCATCA")
'p.Gly3Pro'
```

```pycon
>>> find_variant_string("p.", "ATGGTTGGTTCA", "ATGGGTTCA")
'p.Val2del'
```

```pycon
>>> find_variant_string("p.", "ATGGTTGGTTCA", "ATGGTTGGTGGTTCA")
'p.Gly3dup'
```

```pycon
>>> find_variant_string("p.", "ATGGTTGGTTCA", "ATGGCTGCTTCA")
'p.Val2_Gly3delinsAlaAla'
```

MINUS STRAND

this example is actually comparing TGTAATC and TCTGAAC ...

```pycon
>>> find_variant_string("g.", "GATTACA", "GTTCAGA", minus_strand=True)
'g.[2G>C;3_4insG;6del]'
```

CHECK FOR INVALID INPUTS

```pycon
>>> find_variant_string("g.", "HELLO", "CAT")
Traceback (most recent call last):
 ...
ValueError: Invalid reference sequence
```

```pycon
>>> find_variant_string("g.", "CAT", "HELLO")
Traceback (most recent call last):
 ...
ValueError: Invalid variant sequence
```

CHECK FOR MAX MUTATIONS

```pycon
>>> find_variant_string("g.", "ATTACC", "GATTACA",1)
Traceback (most recent call last):
 ...
countess.utils.variant.TooManyVariationsException: Too many variations (2) in GATTACA
```

modules.name: countess.core.plugins
documentation:
Plugin lifecycle:
  To create a new plugin:
    * Plugin is __init__()ed
    * Plugin.prepare(data) gets called with the connected inputs, to let
      the plugin know what its inputs are.  This method should not do anything
      CPU intensive, it just checks the type of inputs are suitable.
    * Plugin.set_parameter(key, value) gets called, potentially many times.
    * Plugin.prerun() gets called and this should return sample output.
  When inputs have changed:
    * Call prepare() again.
  To change configuration
    * Call Plugin.set_parameter(), potentially several times.
    * Call Plugin.prerun() to generate new output

classes.name: countess.core.plugins.BasePlugin

methods.name: countess.core.plugins.BasePlugin.get_parameter_hash
documentation: Build a hash of all configuration parameters

methods.name: countess.core.plugins.BasePlugin.hash
documentation: Returns a hex digest of the hash of all configuration parameters

methods.name: countess.core.plugins.BasePlugin.preconfigure
documentation:
Called after everything else, to set up any configuration
which may have changed

Base class for all plugins.  Plugins exist as entrypoints, but also
PluginManager checks that plugins subclass this class before accepting them
as plugins.

classes.name: countess.core.plugins.ProcessPlugin

methods.name: countess.core.plugins.ProcessPlugin.preprocess
documentation:
Called with each `data` input from `source` before `process` is called
for that data, to set up config etc.  Can't return anything.

methods.name: countess.core.plugins.ProcessPlugin.process
documentation: Called with each `data` input from `source`, yields results

methods.name: countess.core.plugins.ProcessPlugin.finished
documentation:
Called when a `source` is finished and not able to
send any more messages.  Can be ignored by most things.

methods.name: countess.core.plugins.ProcessPlugin.finalize
documentation:
Called when all sources are finished.  Can be
ignored by most things.  This should reset the
plugin to be ready for another use.

A plugin which accepts data from one or more sources.  Each source is
executed in its own thread and the results are collated through a thread-safe
queue.

methods.name: countess.core.plugins.SimplePlugin.process
documentation:
Called with each `data` input from `source`, calls
`callback` to send messages to the next plugin

classes.name: countess.core.plugins.FileInputPluginFilesMultiParam
documentation:
MultiParam which only asks for one thing, a filename.  Using a MultiParam wrapper
because that makes it easier to extend the FileInputPlugin.

methods.name: countess.core.plugins.FileInputPlugin.read_file_to_dataframe
documentation:
May be called from multiple processes at once.  Note that the 'filename' parameter
overrides the 'file_param.filename.value' as the latter may be a glob.

methods.name: countess.core.plugins.PandasConcatProcessPlugin.__init__
documentation: Like PandsaProcessPlugin but collect all the inputs together before trying to do anything
with them.

methods.name: countess.core.plugins.PandasConcatProcessPlugin.process_dataframe
documentation: Override this to process a single dataframe

classes.name: countess.core.plugins.PandasSimplePlugin

methods.name: countess.core.plugins.PandasSimplePlugin.process
documentation: Just deal with each dataframe as it comes.  PandasSimplePlugins don't care about `source`.

methods.name: countess.core.plugins.PandasSimplePlugin.process_dataframe
documentation: Override this to process a single dataframe

Base class for plugins which accept and return pandas DataFrames.
Subclassing this hides all the distracting aspects of the pipeline
from the plugin implementor, who only needs to override process_dataframe

classes.name: countess.core.plugins.PandasProductPlugin
documentation:
Some plugins need to have all the data from two sources presented to them,
which is tricky in a pipelined environment.  This superclass handles the two
input sources and calls .process_dataframes with pairs of dataframes.
It is currently only used by JoinPlugin
class MapReduceFinalizePlugin(BasePlugin):
def map(self, data) -> Iterable:
return []
def reduce(self, data: Iterable):
pass
def finalize(self, data: Iterable):
pass
class PandasMapReduceFinalizePlugin(MapReduceFinalizePlugin):
def map(self, data) -> Iterable[pd.DataFrame]:
return []
def reduce(self, data: Iterable[pd.DataFrame]) -> pd.DataFrame:
return pd.DataFrame()
def finalize(self, data: pd.DataFrame):
pass

classes.name: countess.core.plugins.PandasTransformBasePlugin
documentation: Base classes for the nine (!!) PandasTransformXToXPlugin superclasses.
XXX this might be excessively DRY but we'll see.
--> PandasTransformAToXMixin --
/       dataframe_to_series     \
PandasTransformBasePlugin --                                 --> PandasTransformAToBMixin
\       series_to_dataframe     /
--> PandasTransformXToBMixin --
A is one of Single (takes a value), Row (takes a pd.Series) or Dict (takes a dict)
B is one of Single (returns a value), Tuple (returns a tuple) or Dict (returns a dict)
Looked at from the point of view of the data it looks like:
P_T_BasePlugin       P_T_AToXMixin            P_T_AToBPlugin     P_T_XToBPlugin        PTBP
/-> process_row ---\
process_dataframe -> dataframe_to_series ---> process_value ---> series_to_dataframe -> merge
\-> process_dict --/
Which probably seems overcomplicated but it also seems to work.
Most plugins just need to pick a PandasTransform class and run with it.  A lot of the time it's
going to be PandasTransformSingleToSinglePlugin.

classes.name: countess.core.plugins.PandasTransformSingleToXMixin
documentation:
Transformer which takes a single column, the name of which is specified
in a ColumnChoiceParam called "column"
XXX instead of just asserting the existence of the parameters should we
actually create them in these mixins?

classes.name: countess.core.plugins.PandasTransformRowToXMixin
documentation:
Transformer which takes an entire row. Less efficient than processing
just a single value, but a bit more flexible.

classes.name: countess.core.plugins.PandasTransformDictToXMixin
documentation: Transformer which takes a row as a dictionary

classes.name: countess.core.plugins.PandasTransformXToSingleMixin
documentation:
Transformer which returns a single value, putting into the column specified
by the StringParam called "output"

classes.name: countess.core.plugins.PandasTransformXToTupleMixin
documentation:
Transformer which returns a tuple of values, putting them into columns
specifed by the StringParams "name" in the ArrayParam "output"

classes.name: countess.core.plugins.PandasTransformXToDictMixin
documentation:
Transformer which returns a dictionary of values, putting them into
columns named after the dictionary keys.

classes.name: countess.core.plugins.PandasTransformSingleToSinglePlugin
documentation: Transformer which takes a single column and returns a single value
Nine combinations of the six mixins!

classes.name: countess.core.plugins.PandasTransformSingleToTuplePlugin
documentation: Transformer which takes a single column and returns a tuple of values

classes.name: countess.core.plugins.PandasTransformSingleToDictPlugin
documentation: Transformer which takes a single column and returns a dictionary of values

classes.name: countess.core.plugins.PandasTransformRowToSinglePlugin
documentation: Transformer which takes a whole row and returns a single value

classes.name: countess.core.plugins.PandasTransformRowToTuplePlugin
documentation: Transformer which takes a whole row and returns a tuple of values

classes.name: countess.core.plugins.PandasTransformRowToDictPlugin
documentation: Transformer which takes a whole row and returns a dictionary of values

classes.name: countess.core.plugins.PandasTransformDictToSinglePlugin
documentation: Transformer which takes a whole row and returns a single value

classes.name: countess.core.plugins.PandasTransformDictToTuplePlugin
documentation: Transformer which takes a whole row and returns a tuple of values

classes.name: countess.core.plugins.PandasTransformDictToDictPlugin
documentation: Transformer which takes a whole row and returns a dictionary of values

methods.name: countess.core.plugins.PandasInputFilesPlugin.read_file_to_dataframe
documentation:
May be called from multiple processes at once.  Note that the 'filename' parameter
overrides the 'file_param.filename.value' as the latter may be a glob.

functions.name: countess.utils.parallel.multiprocess_map
documentation:
Pretty much equivalent to:

    def multiprocess_map(function, values, *args, **kwargs):
        yield from interleave_longest(function(v, *args, **kwargs) for v in values)

but runs in multiple processes using an input_queue
to organize `values` and an output_queue to organize the
returned values.  Or to look at it from a different direction, because
`multiprocessing.Pool.imap_unordered` doesn't take `args`.

classes.name: countess.utils.parallel.IterableMultiprocessQueue
documentation:
This connects a multiprocessing.Queue with a multiprocessing.Value
and gives us a queue that multiple reader processes can iterate over and
they'll each get a StopIteration when the Queue is both finished *and*
empty.

classes.name: countess.core.parameters.BaseParam

methods.name: countess.core.parameters.BaseParam.copy
documentation:
Plugins declare their parameters with instances of BaseParam, these
need to be copied so that multiple Plugins (and multiple rows in an
ArrayParam) can have distinct values

class_variables.name: countess.core.parameters.BaseParam.read_only
documentation: XXX deprecated

class_variables.name: countess.core.parameters.BaseParam.value
documentation: XXX deprecated
Represents the parameters which can be set on a plugin.

classes.name: countess.core.parameters.ScalarParam
documentation:
A ScalarParam has a single value (use one of the subclasses below to give
it a type).

methods.name: countess.core.parameters.ScalarWithOperatorsParam.__add__
documentation: Operator Methods which apply to both StringParams and
NumericParams (but not BooleanParam)

classes.name: countess.core.parameters.StringParam

methods.name: countess.core.parameters.StringParam.__len__
documentation: Operator methods which apply only to strings

A parameter representing a single string value.  A number
of builtin methods are reproduced here to allow the parameter to be
used pretty much like a normal string. In some circumstances it may
be necessary to explicitly cast the parameter or use `parameter.value`
property to get the value inside the parameter.

classes.name: countess.core.parameters.TextParam
documentation:
This is mostly just a convenience for the GUI, it marks this as a
long text field and also removes extra blank lines

classes.name: countess.core.parameters.NumericParam

methods.name: countess.core.parameters.NumericParam.__sub__
documentation: Operator methods which apply only to numerics

A parameter representing a single numeric value.  A large number
of builtin methods are reproduced here to allow the parameter to be
used pretty much like a normal number. In some circumstances it may
be necessary to explicitly cast the parameter or use `parameter.value`
property to get the value inside the parameter.

classes.name: countess.core.parameters.StringCharacterSetParam
documentation:
A StringParam limited to characters from `character_set`.
Call `clean_value` to get an acceptable value

classes.name: countess.core.parameters.FileParam
documentation: A StringParam for holding a filename.

classes.name: countess.core.parameters.DictChoiceParam
documentation:
A drop-down menu parameter choosing between options.
Takes a mapping of choices where the key is the choice
and the value is the displayed value.

classes.name: countess.core.parameters.ChoiceParam
documentation:
A drop-down menu parameter choosing between options.
Defaults to 'None'

classes.name: countess.core.parameters.ColumnChoiceParam
documentation:
A ChoiceParam which DaskTransformPlugin knows
it should automatically update with a list of columns

classes.name: countess.core.parameters.HasSubParametersMixin

methods.name: countess.core.parameters.HasSubParametersMixin.__setattr__
documentation:
Intercepts attempts to set parameters to a value and turns them into parameter.set_value.
Any other kind of attribute assignment is passed through.

methods.name: countess.core.parameters.HasSubParametersMixin.__getitem__
documentation: XXX allows dict-like access to params but not sure if this is a bad idea.

Mixin class which handles access to the parameters inside Plugins
and MultiParams.

classes.name: countess.core.parameters.ArrayParam
documentation:
An ArrayParam contains zero or more copies of `param`, which can be a
SimpleParam or a MultiParam.

classes.name: countess.core.parameters.PerColumnArrayParam
documentation:
An ArrayParam where each value in the array corresponds to a column
in the input dataframe, as set by set_column_choices.

classes.name: countess.core.parameters.FileArrayParam
documentation:
FileArrayParam is an ArrayParam arranged per-file.  Using this class
really just marks it as expecting to be populated from an open file
dialog.

methods.name: countess.core.parameters.MultiParam.__getattr__
documentation: attribute-like accessors

methods.name: countess.core.parameters.MultiParam.__setattr__
documentation:
Intercepts attempts to set parameters to a value and turns them into parameter.set_value.
Any other kind of attribute assignment is passed through.

classes.name: countess.core.parameters.TabularMultiParam
documentation:
This is just used to drop a hint to the GUI as to how the MultiParam
is to be presented ... as a hierarchy or as a table ...

classes.name: countess.core.parameters.FramedMultiParam
documentation:
This is just used to drop a hint to the GUI to display the MultiParam
in its own frame
